name: Best Picture Winners
description: Every Academy Award Best Picture winner, from Wings at the first ceremony (1927/28) to One Battle After Another (2025). Each card pairs the film with its director and the story behind the win.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2025?
fields.extra: Paul Thomas Anderson. Won six Oscars, giving Anderson his first wins after decades of nominations; beat Sinners, which had entered with a record 16 nominations.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2024?
fields.extra: Sean Baker. Baker personally collected four Oscars in one night (picture, director, editing, screenplay), a first for a single film.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2023?
fields.extra: Christopher Nolan. Won seven Oscars and beat Barbie, its rival in the Barbenheimer summer phenomenon.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2022?
fields.extra: Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert. Won seven Oscars, and Michelle Yeoh became the first Asian winner of Best Actress.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2021?
fields.extra: Siân Heder. The first streaming film to win Best Picture (Apple TV+); it upset the heavy favorite, The Power of the Dog.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2020?
fields.extra: Chloé Zhao. Zhao became only the second woman, and the first woman of color, to win Best Director.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2019?
fields.extra: Bong Joon Ho. The first non-English-language film ever to win Best Picture; it beat 1917 and Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2018?
fields.extra: Peter Farrelly. A controversial upset over Roma and A Star Is Born.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2017?
fields.extra: Guillermo del Toro. A romance between a mute cleaner and an amphibian creature; it beat Get Out, Lady Bird, and Dunkirk.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2016?
fields.extra: Barry Jenkins. Announced only after the envelope mixup that first named La La Land as the winner.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2015?
fields.extra: Tom McCarthy. Beat The Revenant and Mad Max: Fury Road while winning only one other Oscar, for Original Screenplay.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2014?
fields.extra: Alejandro G. Iñárritu. Full title: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance). Edited to look like one continuous shot, it beat Boyhood.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2013?
fields.extra: Steve McQueen. The first Best Picture winner from a Black director; it beat Gravity, which took seven Oscars that night.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2012?
fields.extra: Ben Affleck. Won even though Affleck was snubbed for Best Director; it beat Lincoln and Life of Pi.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2011?
fields.extra: Michel Hazanavicius. The first nearly silent winner since Wings at the very first ceremony, and the first French production to win.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2010?
fields.extra: Tom Hooper. Beat The Social Network in one of the decade's most debated face-offs.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2009?
fields.extra: Kathryn Bigelow. Bigelow became the first woman to win Best Director, beating Avatar, directed by her ex-husband James Cameron.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2008?
fields.extra: Danny Boyle. Won eight Oscars for a film that had nearly been released straight to DVD.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2007?
fields.extra: Joel Coen and Ethan Coen. Beat There Will Be Blood in a landmark year for American film.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2006?
fields.extra: Martin Scorsese. Finally won Scorsese Best Director; it was also the first remake to win, based on Hong Kong's Infernal Affairs.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2005?
fields.extra: Paul Haggis. Famously upset the heavy favorite Brokeback Mountain.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2004?
fields.extra: Clint Eastwood. Eastwood's second Best Picture as director; it beat Scorsese's The Aviator.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2003?
fields.back: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
fields.extra: Peter Jackson. Went 11 for 11, tying the record for most wins with Ben-Hur and Titanic; it was the first fantasy film to win.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2002?
fields.extra: Rob Marshall. The first musical to win since Oliver! in 1968; it beat The Pianist and Gangs of New York.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2001?
fields.extra: Ron Howard. Beat Moulin Rouge! and The Fellowship of the Ring.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 2000?
fields.extra: Ridley Scott. Beat Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon and Traffic; Russell Crowe won Best Actor as Maximus.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1999?
fields.extra: Sam Mendes. Won for Mendes's feature directing debut; it beat The Sixth Sense and The Green Mile.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1998?
fields.extra: John Madden. Famously upset Saving Private Ryan after an aggressive Miramax awards campaign.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1997?
fields.extra: James Cameron. Won 11 Oscars, tying Ben-Hur's record, while reigning as the highest-grossing film ever made.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1996?
fields.extra: Anthony Minghella. Won nine Oscars; it beat Fargo and Jerry Maguire.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1995?
fields.extra: Mel Gibson. Beat Apollo 13 and the talking-pig fable Babe.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1994?
fields.extra: Robert Zemeckis. Beat Pulp Fiction and The Shawshank Redemption.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1993?
fields.extra: Steven Spielberg. Spielberg's first Best Director win, for a black-and-white Holocaust drama that beat The Piano and The Fugitive.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1992?
fields.extra: Clint Eastwood. Only the third Western ever to win, after Cimarron and Dances with Wolves.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1991?
fields.extra: Jonathan Demme. The only horror thriller to win Best Picture, and just the third film to sweep the Big Five awards.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1990?
fields.extra: Kevin Costner. Costner's directing debut; it famously beat Goodfellas.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1989?
fields.extra: Bruce Beresford. Won without Beresford even being nominated for Best Director, the same year Do the Right Thing missed a Best Picture nod.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1988?
fields.extra: Barry Levinson. The year's highest-grossing film; Dustin Hoffman won Best Actor as Raymond Babbitt.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1987?
fields.extra: Bernardo Bertolucci. Won all nine of its nominations; it was the first Western feature allowed to film inside Beijing's Forbidden City.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1986?
fields.extra: Oliver Stone. Drawn from Stone's own combat service in Vietnam; it beat Hannah and Her Sisters.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1985?
fields.extra: Sydney Pollack. Beat The Color Purple, which went home empty-handed despite 11 nominations.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1984?
fields.extra: Milos Forman. Won eight Oscars; F. Murray Abraham's Salieri beat his own co-star Tom Hulce for Best Actor.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1983?
fields.extra: James L. Brooks. Brooks personally won picture, director, and screenplay; it beat The Right Stuff.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1982?
fields.extra: Richard Attenborough. Beat E.T. and Tootsie; Ben Kingsley won Best Actor in the title role.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1981?
fields.extra: Hugh Hudson. Upset Warren Beatty's Reds; its Vangelis synthesizer theme became iconic.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1980?
fields.extra: Robert Redford. Redford's directing debut; it famously beat Raging Bull.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1979?
fields.extra: Robert Benton. Beat Apocalypse Now; Dustin Hoffman and Meryl Streep both won acting Oscars.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1978?
fields.extra: Michael Cimino. Beat fellow Vietnam drama Coming Home; Christopher Walken won for its harrowing Russian roulette scenes.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1977?
fields.extra: Woody Allen. Famously beat Star Wars.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1976?
fields.extra: John G. Avildsen. Beat Taxi Driver, Network, and All the President's Men, one of the deepest fields in Oscar history.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1975?
fields.back: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
fields.extra: Milos Forman. The second film to sweep the Big Five (picture, director, actor, actress, screenplay); it beat Jaws.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1974?
fields.extra: Francis Ford Coppola. The first sequel to win Best Picture; it beat Chinatown.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1973?
fields.extra: George Roy Hill. The Paul Newman and Robert Redford con-man caper beat The Exorcist.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1972?
fields.extra: Francis Ford Coppola. Marlon Brando refused his Best Actor Oscar; the film beat Cabaret, which won eight awards that same night.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1971?
fields.extra: William Friedkin. The first R-rated film to win Best Picture; it beat A Clockwork Orange.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1970?
fields.extra: Franklin J. Schaffner. George C. Scott refused his Best Actor Oscar, the first actor ever to do so; the film beat M*A*S*H.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1969?
fields.extra: John Schlesinger. The only X-rated film ever to win Best Picture.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1968?
fields.extra: Carol Reed. The last G-rated winner; the musical triumphed the year 2001: A Space Odyssey was not even nominated for Best Picture.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1967?
fields.back: In the Heat of the Night
fields.extra: Norman Jewison. The Sidney Poitier mystery beat The Graduate and Bonnie and Clyde.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1966?
fields.extra: Fred Zinnemann. Paul Scofield won Best Actor as Sir Thomas More; the film beat Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1965?
fields.extra: Robert Wise. Beat Doctor Zhivago and soon overtook Gone with the Wind as the highest-grossing film ever.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1964?
fields.extra: George Cukor. Beat Mary Poppins; Audrey Hepburn's songs were secretly dubbed by Marni Nixon.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1963?
fields.extra: Tony Richardson. The bawdy British romp remains one of the few outright comedies ever to win.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1962?
fields.extra: David Lean. Beat To Kill a Mockingbird; the desert epic has no women in speaking roles.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1961?
fields.extra: Robert Wise and Jerome Robbins. The first co-directed winner; its 10 Oscars are still the record for a musical.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1960?
fields.extra: Billy Wilder. Wilder took picture, director, and screenplay in a single night for the bittersweet office comedy.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1959?
fields.extra: William Wyler. The first film to win 11 Oscars, a record later tied only by Titanic and The Return of the King.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1958?
fields.extra: Vincente Minnelli. Went nine for nine, a perfect sweep that was briefly the most wins by any film.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1957?
fields.back: The Bridge on the River Kwai
fields.extra: David Lean. Its blacklisted screenwriters went uncredited; the writing Oscar was accepted under the name of novelist Pierre Boulle, who barely spoke English.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1956?
fields.extra: Michael Anderson. Producer Mike Todd's star-stuffed travelogue beat Giant and The Ten Commandments.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1955?
fields.extra: Delbert Mann. The shortest Best Picture winner ever, and one of only two films (with Parasite) to win both the Oscar and the Palme d'Or.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1954?
fields.extra: Elia Kazan. Won eight Oscars; Marlon Brando's "I coulda been a contender" speech became legend.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1953?
fields.extra: Fred Zinnemann. Won eight Oscars, tying Gone with the Wind's record at the time.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1952?
fields.extra: Cecil B. DeMille. Beat High Noon and is routinely called the weakest winner ever; Singin' in the Rain was not even nominated.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1951?
fields.extra: Vincente Minnelli. The Gershwin musical upset A Streetcar Named Desire and A Place in the Sun.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1950?
fields.extra: Joseph L. Mankiewicz. Its 14 nominations stood as the record for 75 years, tied by Titanic and La La Land before Sinners finally broke it.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1949?
fields.extra: Robert Rossen. Broderick Crawford won Best Actor as Willie Stark, the demagogue modeled on Huey Long.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1948?
fields.extra: Laurence Olivier. The first British film to win; Olivier also took Best Actor, becoming the first person to direct himself to an acting Oscar.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1947?
fields.extra: Elia Kazan. Its exposé of polite American antisemitism beat Miracle on 34th Street and Great Expectations.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1946?
fields.back: The Best Years of Our Lives
fields.extra: William Wyler. Beat It's a Wonderful Life; real WWII veteran and double amputee Harold Russell won two Oscars for the same role.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1945?
fields.extra: Billy Wilder. The unflinching alcoholism drama also shared the top prize at the first Cannes Film Festival.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1944?
fields.extra: Leo McCarey. Bing Crosby's singing priest beat the noir classic Double Indemnity.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1943?
fields.extra: Michael Curtiz. Made as just another studio picture, it beat For Whom the Bell Tolls and became perhaps the best-loved winner of all.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1942?
fields.extra: William Wyler. The home-front drama was such potent wartime propaganda that Churchill reportedly called it worth a hundred battleships.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1941?
fields.extra: John Ford. Famously beat Citizen Kane.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1940?
fields.extra: Alfred Hitchcock. The only Hitchcock film to win Best Picture (he never won Best Director); it beat The Grapes of Wrath and The Philadelphia Story.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1939?
fields.extra: Victor Fleming. Beat The Wizard of Oz, Mr. Smith Goes to Washington, and Stagecoach in Hollywood's fabled year; Hattie McDaniel became the first Black Oscar winner.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1938?
fields.back: You Can't Take It with You
fields.extra: Frank Capra. Capra's third Best Director win in five years; it beat Grand Illusion, the first foreign-language Best Picture nominee.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1937?
fields.extra: William Dieterle. The biopic centers on the Dreyfus affair, yet the word Jew is never spoken aloud in it.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1936?
fields.extra: Robert Z. Leonard. The three-hour Broadway biopic; Luise Rainer won the first of her back-to-back Best Actress Oscars.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1935?
fields.extra: Frank Lloyd. The last film to win Best Picture and nothing else; its three Best Actor nominees prompted the creation of the supporting categories.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1934?
fields.extra: Frank Capra. The first film to sweep the Big Five: picture, director, actor, actress, and screenplay.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1932/33?
fields.extra: Frank Lloyd. The Noël Coward pageant of British life is often ranked among the most forgotten winners.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1931/32?
fields.extra: Edmund Goulding. The only Best Picture winner never nominated for any other Oscar; Garbo's "I want to be alone" comes from it.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1930/31?
fields.extra: Wesley Ruggles. The first Western to win; no other Western won until Dances with Wolves 59 years later.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1929/30?
fields.back: All Quiet on the Western Front
fields.extra: Lewis Milestone. The pacifist WWI epic was later banned in Germany by the Nazis.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1928/29?
fields.extra: Harry Beaumont. The first sound film and the first musical to win Best Picture.

fields.front: Which film won the Best Picture Oscar for 1927/28?
fields.extra: William A. Wellman. The only fully silent winner until The Artist; at that first ceremony Sunrise won a separate top award, Unique and Artistic Picture, never given again.
